extra.message: User-controlled `name` query parameter (line 19) is interpolated into an f-string on line 38 and passed to Jinja2 Template().render() on line 41. This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI): an attacker can inject Jinja2 expressions like {{config.__class__.__init__.__globals__['os'].popen('id').read()}} to achieve Remote Code Execution. The f-string interpolates name into the HTML string first, then Template().render() evaluates any Jinja2 syntax within it.

User-controlled `name` query parameter is directly interpolated into an f-string and returned as HTML content on line 38 without any escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the `name` parameter (e.g., `/?name=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>`). The response_class=HTMLResponse (line 18) makes the browser render it as HTML, enabling reflected Cross-Site Scripting.

The response from an external IP service (config.PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL) is fetched on line 31 and interpolated directly into HTML on line 38 without sanitization. If the external service is compromised or returns attacker-controlled content (e.g., via a man-in-the-middle attack or DNS spoofing), the response text could contain malicious HTML/JavaScript that would be rendered in the user's browser.
Hardcoded secret value `SUPER_SECRET_NAME = "John Ripper"` instead of reading from environment variable (as indicated by the FIXME comment). This exposes a sensitive credential in source code.
Hardcoded secret value `SUPER_SECRET_TOKEN = "5u93R53Cr3tT0k3n"` instead of reading from environment variable (as indicated by the FIXME comment). This exposes a sensitive credential in source code.

FastAPI debug mode is set from config.DEBUG which reads from the `DEBUG` environment variable. The .env_temp file sets DEBUG=True (line 3), meaning debug mode may be active in production. This enables detailed error pages and tracebacks that can leak sensitive application internals, source code, stack traces, and configuration values.

The `public_ip` is fetched from `PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL` which is loaded from an environment variable. If an attacker can control the environment variable (e.g., via a compromised CI/CD pipeline or container runtime), they could make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/ for cloud metadata, or internal APIs on localhost). The response is then reflected in the HTML output, enabling both SSRF and information disclosure.
